FirstInformationReport._format: Semi-structured — police-issued, format varies by state, target extraction
FirstInformationReport._note: FIR format is governed by CrPC Section 154 but layout, language, and fields vary significantly by state. Core fields (FIR number, section, date, incident) are consistent. Extract all available — null for absent.

LegalSectionsInvoked._note: IPC sections e.g. 302, 304A, 307 — array as comma-separated string

Description._note: Full narration of the incident as written in FIR

Vehicle._note: Populated only for road/rail/air accident cases

Accused._comment: Array — one entry per accused person if named

Witnesses._comment: Array — one entry per witness named in FIR

DeathCategory._note: Always UNNATURAL for FIR documents — set by default, confirm from NatureOfIncident

InquestPanchananaReport._format: Semi-structured — police/magistrate issued under CrPC Section 174/176, format varies by state
InquestPanchananaReport._note: Inquest is conducted at scene of death by police (S.174 CrPC) or magistrate (S.176 CrPC for suspicious deaths). Panchanama is the scene observation report. Both captured here as they are often a single combined document.

ReportType._note: S.174 CrPC = Police Inquest, S.176 CrPC = Magistrate Inquest

BodyConditionNotes._note: Physical condition of body as observed — rigor mortis, injuries, decomposition etc.

SuspectedCauseOfDeath._note: Officer's preliminary opinion — not final cause

Witnesses._comment: Array — panchas (witnesses) who signed the inquest

DeathCategory._note: Always UNNATURAL — inquest is only conducted for unnatural or suspicious deaths

FinalPoliceInvestigationReport._format: Semi-structured — police issued (chargesheet or closure report), format varies by state
FinalPoliceInvestigationReport._note: Also called Chargesheet (if crime established) or Closure/FR Report (if no crime). Filed under CrPC Section 173. This is the most legally significant police document — it records the final police conclusion after investigation.

ReportType._note: Chargesheet = crime established, ClosureReport = no crime / accidental

FIRNumber._note: Must match FIR number on FIR document — cross-reference check

Summary._note: Brief summary of what investigation found

VisceraCFSLReportNumber._note: Central Forensic Science Laboratory report reference

FinalConclusion._note: Police final conclusion on manner of death — most critical field

Accused._comment: Array — relevant only for murder/homicide cases

CourtReferenceNumber._note: Case number under which report filed in court

DeathCategory._note: Always UNNATURAL — final report is only filed for unnatural/suspicious deaths

PostmortemReport._format: Semi-structured — hospital/forensic dept issued, government format with state variation
PostmortemReport._note: PMR is the most medically authoritative document for unnatural death. Issued by a government medical officer. Format follows MLO (Medico-Legal Officer) guidelines but varies by state hospital. Most critical fields are consistent.

FIRNumber._note: Cross-reference with FIR and Inquest report

RequisitionBy._note: Name and designation of police officer who requisitioned the PM

ExternalInjuries._note: Description of all external injuries — critical for accident vs assault determination

SignificantFindings._note: Extract only findings material to cause of death

SampleTypes._note: Stomach contents, liver, kidney, blood, etc.

ImmediateCause._note: Part I(a) — disease/injury directly causing death

AntecedentCause._note: Part I(b) — condition that caused immediate cause

FinalOpinion._note: Doctor's final stated opinion — most critical field in entire document

PendingViscera._note: True if final cause pending viscera report — changes claim workflow

DeathCategory._note: Always UNNATURAL — PMR is only conducted for unnatural or suspicious deaths

VisceraChemicalExaminationReport._format: Variable — forensic lab issued, format varies significantly by lab and state
VisceraChemicalExaminationReport._note: Issued by FSL (Forensic Science Laboratory) / CFSL after chemical analysis of viscera samples preserved at PMR. Critical for poisoning / drug overdose cases. Format is lab-specific but conclusion section is always present.

PMRReferenceNumber._note: PMR number under which samples were preserved — cross-reference

SamplesAnalyzed._note: List of sample types received — stomach contents, liver, kidney, blood, urine, etc.

SubstancesDetected._note: CRITICAL — any toxic substance, drug, poison, alcohol found. List as comma-separated.

Conclusion._note: Forensic examiner's final conclusion — always present, most critical field

DeathCategory._note: Always UNNATURAL — viscera analysis only conducted for unnatural/suspicious deaths

NewspaperCutting._format: Fully unstructured — no fixed format, supplementary evidence only
NewspaperCutting._note: Newspaper cuttings are supplementary corroborating evidence. No critical fields — a blurry or incomplete newspaper cutting does not block the claim. Extract only what is clearly readable. Everything else goes to UnmappedFields.

DeathCategory._note: Always UNNATURAL — newspaper cuttings submitted only for unnatural death claims
